From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Concept of parliamentary democracy

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Responsible_government "Special:EditPage/Responsible government") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Responsible government"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Responsible+government%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Responsible+government%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Responsible+government%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Responsible+government%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Responsible+government%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Responsible+government%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(December 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Responsible government** is a conception of a [system of government](/wiki/System_of_government "System of government") that embodies the principle of parliamentary [accountability](/wiki/Accountability "Accountability"), the foundation of the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") of [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Governments (the equivalent of the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)")) in Westminster democracies are responsible to parliament rather than to the monarch, or, in a colonial context, to the imperial government, and in a republican context, to the president. If the parliament is [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral"), then the government is usually responsible first to the parliament's [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"), which is more representative than the [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"), as it usually has more members and they are always [directly elected](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election").

Responsible government and the principle of parliamentary accountability manifests itself in several ways. Firstly, [ministers](/wiki/Minister_(government) "Minister (government)") must account to parliament for their decisions and for the performance of their departments. This requirement to make announcements and to answer questions in parliament means that ministers must have the privileges of the floor, which are only granted to those who are members of parliament. Secondly, and most importantly, although ministers are officially appointed by the authority of the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and can theoretically be dismissed at the pleasure of the sovereign, they concurrently retain their office subject to their holding the confidence of the lower house of parliament. When the lower house has passed a [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") in the government, the government must ordinarily immediately resign or submit itself to the electorate in a new general election.

Lastly, the head of state is in turn required to effectuate their executive power only through these responsible ministers. They must never attempt to set up a shadow government of executives or advisors and attempt to use them as instruments of government, or to rely upon their unofficial advice. They generally are not permitted to take any action under the color of their executive power without that action being as a result of the counsel and advisement of their responsible ministers. Common exceptions to this rule include emergency or wartime acts of necessity and the granting of certain state honours. Their ministers are required to counsel them (i.e., explain to them and be sure they understand any issue that they will be called upon to decide) and to form and have recommendations for them (i.e., their [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional) "Advice (constitutional)") or advisement) to choose from, which are the ministers' formal, reasoned recommendations as to what course of action should be taken.

## History in the British Empire and Commonwealth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Responsible_government&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History in the British Empire and Commonwealth")]

From the middle of the 19th century, the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") began to introduce systems of responsible government to the colonial governments of its settler colonies including in Canada, Newfoundland, Australia, New Zealand and South Africa. These systems were strongly based upon the system that had historically developed in the United Kingdom, with a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") legislature and an executive responsible to the Lower House.

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Responsible_government&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/SIMPSON_Legislative_Assembly_of_Lower_Canada.jpg/250px-SIMPSON_Legislative_Assembly_of_Lower_Canada.jpg)](/wiki/File:SIMPSON_Legislative_Assembly_of_Lower_Canada.jpg)

The [Legislative Assembly of Lower Canada](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Lower_Canada "Legislative Assembly of Lower Canada") in 1792. Elective assemblies existed in [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") since the 18th century, although the colonies' executive councils were not beholden to them.

Responsible government was implemented in several colonies of [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") (present day [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")), between 1848 and 1850, with the executive council formulating policy with the assistance of the legislative branch, which voted approval or disapproval, and the appointed governor enacting those policies that the legislature had approved. This replaced the previous system, whereby the governor took advice from an executive council and used the legislature chiefly to raise money.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Under responsible government, the executive is also a part of the legislature. The Executive branch must consist of MPs.
Responsible government was a major element of Canada's gradual development toward independence. The concept of responsible government is associated in Canada more with [self-government](/wiki/Self-governing_colony "Self-governing colony") than with parliamentary accountability; hence, there is the notion that the [Dominion of Newfoundland](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland") "gave up responsible government" when it suspended its self-governing status in 1933, as a result of financial problems. It did not regain responsible government until it became a [province of Canada](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") in 1949.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

#### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Responsible_government&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/John_George_Lambton%2C_1st_Earl_of_Durham_by_Thomas_Phillips.jpg/250px-John_George_Lambton%2C_1st_Earl_of_Durham_by_Thomas_Phillips.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_George_Lambton,_1st_Earl_of_Durham_by_Thomas_Phillips.jpg)

[The Earl of Durham](/wiki/John_Lambton,_1st_Earl_of_Durham "John Lambton, 1st Earl of Durham"), Governor General of British North America, made [a report](/wiki/Report_on_the_Affairs_of_British_North_America_(1839) "Report on the Affairs of British North America (1839)") shortly after the [1837–38 rebellions](/wiki/Rebellions_of_1837%E2%80%931838 "Rebellions of 1837–1838") that recommended the implementation of responsible government.

At first appointed governors and appointed member who filled the governors' executive councils held power. Even after the formation of elected legislative assemblies, starting with [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_House_of_Assembly "Nova Scotia House of Assembly") in 1758, governors and their executive councils did not require the consent of elected legislators in order to carry out all their roles and govern. Only in the decades leading up to [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") in 1867 did the governing councils of those British North American colonies become responsible to the elected representatives of the people.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the aftermath of the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), sparked by the perceived shortcomings of [virtual representation](/wiki/Virtual_representation "Virtual representation"), the British government became sensitive to unrest in its remaining colonies with large populations of European-descended colonists. Elected assemblies were introduced to both [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") and [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada") with the [Constitutional Act 1791](/wiki/Constitutional_Act_1791 "Constitutional Act 1791"). Many reformers called for these assemblies having some control over the executive power, Political unrest and tensions between the governors and assemblies in both Upper and Lower Canada grew.

After the [Rebellions of 1837–1838](/wiki/Rebellions_of_1837%E2%80%931838 "Rebellions of 1837–1838") in [the Canadas](/wiki/The_Canadas "The Canadas"), [the Earl of Durham](/wiki/John_Lambton,_1st_Earl_of_Durham "John Lambton, 1st Earl of Durham") was appointed governor general of British North America, with the task of examining the issues and determining how to defuse tensions. In [his report](/wiki/Report_on_the_Affairs_of_British_North_America_(1839) "Report on the Affairs of British North America (1839)"), he recommended that colonies that were developed enough should be granted "responsible government". This term specifically meant the policy that British-appointed governors should bow to the will of elected colonial assemblies. Elected legislators had some power to rein in the appointed executive council. But representative government was unquestionably achieved when the executive council was formed of members exclusively from the party having a majority in the representative branch of a colonial legislature, a thing achieved in different colonies starting in the late 1840s.

But Durham's views were not shared by everyone -- the [Lieutenant Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Ontario "Lieutenant Governor of Ontario") of Upper Canada, [Sir Francis Bond Head](/wiki/Sir_Francis_Bond_Head "Sir Francis Bond Head"), wrote in one dispatch to London that, if responsible government were implemented, "democracy, in the worst possible form, will prevail in our colonies."[[5]](#cite_note-5)

#### Implementation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Responsible_government&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Implementation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Young_Canada_Delighted_with_Responsible_Government_c092201-v8.jpg/250px-Young_Canada_Delighted_with_Responsible_Government_c092201-v8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Young_Canada_Delighted_with_Responsible_Government_c092201-v8.jpg)

A [political cartoon](/wiki/Political_cartoon "Political cartoon") from *[Punch](/wiki/Punch_(magazine) "Punch (magazine)")* in Canada, in 1849, depicting a "young Canada" being delighted by [Lord Elgin](/wiki/James_Bruce,_8th_Earl_of_Elgin "James Bruce, 8th Earl of Elgin") pulling the strings of a puppet, representing responsible government

The first instance of responsible government in the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") outside of the United Kingdom itself was achieved by the colony of [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") in January and February 1848, through the efforts of [Joseph Howe](/wiki/Joseph_Howe "Joseph Howe"). Howe's push for responsible government was inspired by the work of [Thomas McCulloch](/wiki/Thomas_McCulloch "Thomas McCulloch") and [Jotham Blanchard](/wiki/Jotham_Blanchard "Jotham Blanchard") almost two decades earlier.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The [plaque](/wiki/Commemorative_plaque "Commemorative plaque") erected by the [Historic Sites and Monuments Board of Canada](/wiki/Historic_Sites_and_Monuments_Board_of_Canada "Historic Sites and Monuments Board of Canada") in the Nova Scotia House of Assembly reads:

> First Responsible Government in the British Empire.  
> The first [executive council](/wiki/Executive_Council_(Commonwealth_countries) "Executive Council (Commonwealth countries)") chosen exclusively from the party having a majority in the representative branch of a colonial legislature was formed in Nova Scotia on 2 February 1848. Following a vote of want of confidence in the preceding council, [James Boyle Uniacke](/wiki/James_Boyle_Uniacke "James Boyle Uniacke"), who had moved the resolution, became attorney general and leader of the government. Joseph Howe, the long-time campaigner for this "peaceable revolution", became [provincial secretary](/wiki/Provincial_secretary "Provincial secretary"). Other members of the council were Hugh Bell, Wm.F. Desbarres, Lawrence O.C. Doyle, Herbert Huntingdon, James McNab, Michael Tobin, and George R. Young.

The colony of [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") soon followed in May 1848,[[8]](#cite_note-8) when lieutenant governor [Edmund Walker Head](/wiki/Edmund_Walker_Head "Edmund Walker Head") brought in a more balanced representation of members of the Legislative Assembly to the Executive Council and ceded more powers to that body.

In the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), responsible government was introduced when the governor general, [Lord Elgin](/wiki/James_Bruce,_8th_Earl_of_Elgin "James Bruce, 8th Earl of Elgin"), swore in the ministry of [Louis-Hippolyte Lafontaine](/wiki/Louis-Hippolyte_Lafontaine "Louis-Hippolyte Lafontaine") and [Robert Baldwin](/wiki/Robert_Baldwin "Robert Baldwin") on 11 March 1848. It was put to the test in the following year, when Reformers in the legislature passed the [Rebellion Losses Bill](/wiki/Rebellion_Losses_Bill "Rebellion Losses Bill"), which provided compensation to French-Canadians who suffered losses during the Rebellions of 1837–1838. Elgin had serious misgivings about the bill, but, nonetheless [assented](/wiki/Royal_Assent "Royal Assent") to it, despite demands from the Tories that he refuse to do so. Elgin was physically assaulted by an English-speaking mob for this and the parliament building in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") was burned to the ground in the [ensuing riots](/wiki/Montreal_Riots "Montreal Riots"). Nonetheless, the Rebellion Losses Bill helped entrench responsible government into Canadian politics.

In time, the granting of responsible government became the first step on the road to complete independence. Canada gradually gained greater autonomy through inter imperial and Commonwealth diplomacy, including the [British North America Act, 1867](/wiki/British_North_America_Act,_1867 "British North America Act, 1867"); the [Statute of Westminster, 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster,_1931 "Statute of Westminster, 1931"); and the patriation of the [Constitution Act, 1982](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982").

### Australia and New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Responsible_government&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Australia and New Zealand")]

Prior to European colonisation, the [Australian continent](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)") was inhabited by [Aboriginal](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") and [Torres Strait Island](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islands "Torres Strait Islands") peoples, who had their own traditional forms of self-government. They were divided into various nations and clans and, in some cases, large alliances between several nations. In 1770, [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") officer [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") sailed along the east coast of Australia and claimed it for King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"). Nearly two decades later, in 1788 the [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") led by [Arthur Phillip](/wiki/Arthur_Phillip "Arthur Phillip") established the first permanent European settlement in Australia at [Port Jackson](/wiki/Port_Jackson "Port Jackson"). The settlers gradually expanded across the continent, displacing the indigenous population, until they had established six colonies; the legal system of these colonies were based on the [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") system.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At first, the Australian colonies were run by autocratic governors. They were appointed by the [British monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), but in practice the governors exercised vast executive and legislative powers with very little oversight. Several factors influenced this: most of these early colonies were penal colonies, they were at a great distance from the United Kingdom and communication was slow. In addition the continent was of enormous size and the Australian colonies were largely unexplored by Europeans and sparsely settled by them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1808 a military [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") known as the [Rum Rebellion](/wiki/Rum_Rebellion "Rum Rebellion") deposed [Governor Bligh](/wiki/Governor_Bligh "Governor Bligh") and briefly established military rule over the [Colony of New South Wales](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales") until a new Governor ([Lachlan Macquarie](/wiki/Lachlan_Macquarie "Lachlan Macquarie")) was appointed and sent from Britain. This did not aid in establishing responsible or representative government.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/William_Charles_Wentworth_%28sepia%29.jpg/250px-William_Charles_Wentworth_%28sepia%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Charles_Wentworth_(sepia).jpg)

[William Charles Wentworth](/wiki/William_Wentworth "William Wentworth") was the leading advocate for responsible government in the Australian colonies and was central in the establishment of the [Parliament of New South Wales](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales").[[9]](#cite_note-Tink22-9)

The early colonists, coming mostly from the United Kingdom (which by 1801 included Ireland), were familiar with the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") and made efforts to reform local government in order to increase the opportunity for ordinary men to participate. The Governors and London therefore set in motion a gradual process of establishing a Westminster system in the colonies. They did not want to get ahead of population or economic growth, nor be so slow as to provoke clamouring for revolutionary change as had happened in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") and was threatened in the [Rebellions of 1837–1838](/wiki/Rebellions_of_1837%E2%80%931838 "Rebellions of 1837–1838") in Canada. The government first established appointed or partially elected Legislative Councils. [William Wentworth](/wiki/William_Wentworth "William Wentworth") among other prominent colonists in this time advocated for the introduction of responsible government in the Australian colonies.[[10]](#cite_note-Tink2-10)

Violence erupted in the Colony of Victoria during the 1850s, where there was growing political discontent and civil disobedience among the common men, especially in the inland gold fields areas. This culminated in the 1854 [Eureka Rebellion](/wiki/Eureka_Rebellion "Eureka Rebellion") near [Ballarat](/wiki/Ballarat "Ballarat"). 190 miners armed themselves, erected a [stockade](/wiki/Stockade "Stockade"), and raised the Southern Cross Flag. They demanded an end to taxation (via an expensive miner's license) without representation, the right to vote in colonial elections, and colonial maintenance and upkeep of the area's roads.

The British colonial forces overran the stockade, capturing or killing dozens of miners and bringing many to [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") for trial. However, mass public support led to the miners' release. Within a year, most of their demands had been met, including responsible self-government and universal male suffrage for the Colony of Victoria.

The Eureka Rebellion and events in Victoria resonated around the Australian colonies, which had their own regional agitators for change. South Australia was quick to pass universal male suffrage, and Victoria and New South Wales followed soon after. By the end of the 1850s, all the Australian colonies and New Zealand had achieved responsible self-government. Only Western Australia took until 1890 to achieve this.

The [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") was originally part of South Australia, but transferred to the Australian Commonwealth Government in 1911. It then *lost* responsible self-government (although residents could still vote in federal elections) and did not gain it back until 1974. Likewise, the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") was originally part of New South Wales. It was transferred to the Commonwealth Government in 1911 and lost responsible self-government until 1989.

#### Participation by women and indigenous people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Responsible_government&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Participation by women and indigenous people")]

See also: [Women's suffrage in Australia](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_Australia "Women's suffrage in Australia") and [Women's suffrage in New Zealand](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_New_Zealand "Women's suffrage in New Zealand")

White female suffrage was gained from the 1890s to the 1910s in Australia and New Zealand (1893), allowing the other half of the ethnic European population to participate in responsible representative government.

In New Zealand, [the indigenous Maori became British subjects](/wiki/New_Zealand_nationality_law "New Zealand nationality law") following the 1840 [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi").

In Australia during the colonial period, some Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people may have theoretically had the right to vote in colonial elections. But, in practice they were usually unable to exercise this right: they lived traditional lifestyles in remote areas [uncontacted](/wiki/Uncontacted_people "Uncontacted people") by the colony, or affected by the disruption of the [Frontier Wars](/wiki/Australian_frontier_wars "Australian frontier wars"). In addition, they often were excluded from voting by racial discrimination in practices or by property requirements.

After federation, the [Commonwealth Franchise Act 1902](/wiki/Commonwealth_Franchise_Act_1902 "Commonwealth Franchise Act 1902") barred Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people from voting in federal elections unless they were already eligible to vote in their state. Queensland explicitly barred Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people from voting until the 1960s, while Western Australia barred Aboriginal people unless they successfully applied to become citizens. As a result, most[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Aboriginal people across the country were prevented from voting, including those who in theory had the right. Later, the 1962 Commonwealth Electoral Act gave all Aboriginal people the option to enroll to vote, but still most were not able to exercise their rights.

After passage of the [1967 Australian referendum](/wiki/1967_Australian_referendum_(Aboriginals) "1967 Australian referendum (Aboriginals)"), the federal government was allowed to count Indigenous Australians in the census, and to ensure that their voting rights were respected across the country.

### Cape Colony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Responsible_government&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cape Colony")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/1_John_Molteno_-_1st_Prime_Minister_of_the_Cape_-_1860s_-_Copy2.jpg/250px-1_John_Molteno_-_1st_Prime_Minister_of_the_Cape_-_1860s_-_Copy2.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_John_Molteno_-_1st_Prime_Minister_of_the_Cape_-_1860s_-_Copy2.jpg)

[John Molteno](/wiki/John_Molteno "John Molteno"), Father of Responsible Government and first [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister "Prime Minister") of the Cape

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/John_Molteno_and_Saul_Solomon_preside_over_Cape_Government_-_Zingari_cartoon_1873.jpg/250px-John_Molteno_and_Saul_Solomon_preside_over_Cape_Government_-_Zingari_cartoon_1873.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Molteno_and_Saul_Solomon_preside_over_Cape_Government_-_Zingari_cartoon_1873.jpg)

Cartoon critical of responsible government, showing power and positions divided between various factions by Cape leaders, depicted as owls, [Molteno](/wiki/John_Charles_Molteno "John Charles Molteno") (to the right) and [Saul Solomon](/wiki/Saul_Solomon "Saul Solomon")

The [Cape Colony](/wiki/British_Cape_Colony "British Cape Colony"), in Southern Africa, was under responsible self-government from 1872 until 1910 when it became the [Cape Province](/wiki/Cape_Province "Cape Province") of the new [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Under its previous system of [representative government](/wiki/Representative_government "Representative government"), the Ministers of the Cape Government reported directly to the [British Imperial Governor](/wiki/British_Cape_Colony "British Cape Colony"), and not to the locally elected representatives in the [Cape Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Cape_of_Good_Hope "Parliament of the Cape of Good Hope"). Among Cape citizens of all races, growing anger at their powerlessness in influencing unpopular imperial decisions had repeatedly led to protests and rowdy political meetings – especially during the early "[Convict Crisis](/wiki/Convict_Crisis "Convict Crisis")" of 1849 to 1850.

A popular political movement for responsible government soon emerged, under local leader [John Molteno](/wiki/John_Molteno "John Molteno"). A protracted struggle was then conducted over the ensuing years as the movement (known informally as "the responsibles") grew increasingly powerful, and used their parliamentary majority to put pressure on the British Governor, withholding public finances from him, and conducting public agitations. Not everyone favoured responsible government though, and pro-imperial press outlets even accused the movement of constituting "crafts and assaults of the devil".[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Supporters believed that the most effective means of instituting responsible government was simply to change the section of the constitution which prevented government officials from being elected to parliament or members of parliament from serving in executive positions. The conflict therefore centred on the changing of this specific section. "Although responsible government merely required an amendment to s.79 of the constitution, it transpired only after nearly twenty years in 1872 when the so-called 'responsibles' under Molteno were able to command sufficient support in both houses to secure the passage of the necessary bill."[[13]](#cite_note-13) Finally, with a parliamentary majority and with the Colonial Office and new Governor [Henry Barkly](/wiki/Henry_Barkly "Henry Barkly") won over, Molteno instituted responsible government, making the Ministers directly responsible to the Cape Parliament, and becoming the Cape's first [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister "Prime Minister").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The ensuing period saw an economic recovery, a massive growth in exports and an expansion of the colony's frontiers. Despite political complications that arose from time to time (such as an ill-fated scheme by the [British Colonial Office](/wiki/British_Colonial_Office "British Colonial Office") to enforce a [confederation](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation") in Southern Africa in 1878, and tensions with the [Afrikaner](/wiki/Afrikaner "Afrikaner")-dominated [Government of Transvaal](/wiki/South_African_Republic "South African Republic") over trade and railroad construction), economic and social progress in the [Cape Colony](/wiki/British_Cape_Colony "British Cape Colony") continued at a steady pace until a renewed attempt to extend British control over the hinterland caused the outbreak of the [Anglo-Boer Wars](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War") in 1899.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

An important feature of the [Cape Colony](/wiki/British_Cape_Colony "British Cape Colony") under responsible government was that it was the only state in southern Africa (and one of very few in the world at the time) to have a [non-racial system of voting](/wiki/Cape_Qualified_Franchise "Cape Qualified Franchise").
[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)
Later however – following the [South Africa Act 1909](/wiki/South_Africa_Act_1909 "South Africa Act 1909") to form the [Union of South Africa](/wiki/Union_of_South_Africa "Union of South Africa") – this [multi-racial universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") was steadily eroded, and eventually abolished by the [Apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") government in 1948.

* 1848 – [Province of New Brunswick](/wiki/Province_of_New_Brunswick "Province of New Brunswick")

* 1856 – [Colony of New Zealand](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand "Colony of New Zealand") and the [Colony of Tasmania](/wiki/Colony_of_Tasmania "Colony of Tasmania")

* 1890 – [Colony of Western Australia](/wiki/Colony_of_Western_Australia "Colony of Western Australia")

* 1907 – [Orange River Colony](/wiki/Orange_River_Colony "Orange River Colony")

## In German history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Responsible_government&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In German history")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Responsible_government "Special:EditPage/Responsible government") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(May 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In the early 1860s, the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") Prime Minister [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") was involved in a bitter dispute with the Liberals, who sought to institute a system of responsible government modeled on that of Britain. Bismarck, who strongly opposed that demand, managed to deflect the pressure by embarking energetically and successfully on the [unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany"). The Liberals, who were also strong German nationalists, backed Bismarck's unification efforts and tacitly accepted that the Constitution of Imperial Germany, crafted by Bismarck, did not include a responsible government – the Chancellor being accountable solely to the emperor and needing no parliamentary confidence. Germany gained a responsible government only with the [German constitutional reforms of October 1918](/wiki/German_constitutional_reforms_of_October_1918 "German constitutional reforms of October 1918"); this was affirmed under the [new republican constitution](/wiki/Weimar_Constitution "Weimar Constitution") of the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") and made more secure by the [basic law](/wiki/Basic_Law_of_Germany "Basic Law of Germany") of the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Federal Republic of Germany"). Historians account the lack of responsible government in the formative decades of united Germany as one of the factors contributing to the prolonged weakness of German democratic institutions, lasting also after such a government was finally instituted.

From 1934 to 1949, Newfoundland was governed by the [Newfoundland Commission of Government](/wiki/Newfoundland_Commission_of_Government "Newfoundland Commission of Government"), a body that was subordinate to the [British government](/wiki/British_government "British government"). Responsible government in Newfoundland was restored in 1949, after it joined the [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation").
